name: Beaver Lake
short_description: A scenic lake rockhounding site in central Pennsylvania where gentle shores reveal quartz, feldspar, and mica specimens. Accessible year-round with minimal crowds.
description:
Beaver Lake in Penn Township, Lycoming County, offers rockhounders an accessible location along the gentle shoreline of this scenic water body. The site sits at 908 feet elevation with predominantly gentle slopes, making it suitable for casual collectors and families. The lake's erosional action continuously exposes fresh mineral specimens along the beach and shallow water margins.

The geology of this region reflects Pennsylvania's complex Appalachian basement rocks, where ancient metamorphic and igneous formations contain abundant quartz, feldspar, and mica minerals. The lake itself was created by glacial activity and damming, and the ongoing erosion of surrounding hillsides brings mineral-rich sediments to the shoreline. Quartz varieties are particularly common, appearing as milky white, translucent, and occasionally smoky specimens.

Expect to find smaller to medium-sized specimens rather than large crystals. The shoreline produces the best results, especially after seasonal weather events that churn up new material. Feldspar fragments often display iridescent qualities in certain light, while mica sheets can be found in various sizes.

This location is best visited during temperate months when lake access is easiest and the shoreline is most exposed. The gentle terrain makes it ideal for introducing children to rockhounding without the physical demands of hiking to remote sites. Pack a field guide to identify the various quartz types and feldspar species you'll encounter.
history: This region lies within the Appalachian Mountains of Pennsylvania, where Precambrian metamorphic basement rocks containing abundant feldspar and quartz were uplifted and exposed by tectonic activity over 300 million years ago. Beaver Lake itself is a more recent feature, created by glacial activity during the Pleistocene epoch that carved valleys and allowed for subsequent dam construction, which continues to expose fresh mineral material along its shorelines today.
tips: Search the immediate shoreline and shallow margins where water action has eroded the banks and deposited mineral fragments. Use a small geology pick or flathead screwdriver to carefully work exposed patches along the water line, and a mesh bag to carry specimens without damaging them. Look for white and translucent quartz pieces, glassy feldspar fragments with visible cleavage planes, and thin mica sheets that often catch the light - specimens here typically range from half an inch to three inches.
terrain_notes: Elevation 908 feet with gentle slope throughout the shoreline approach. Terrain is relatively flat with sandy and rocky beach areas. Walking distance from parking to best collecting areas is typically under half a mile. Footing is variable - mix of sand, gravel, and smooth lake rocks, requiring sturdy footwear. Some areas may be muddy depending on recent water levels and weather.
written_directions: From Williamsport (approximately 10 miles south), take PA Route 15 north toward Muncy. Continue north for about 8 miles, then turn northeast onto local roads toward Penn Township following signs for Beaver Lake recreational area. Parking and lake access points are marked near the lake proper. GPS coordinates (41.295518, -76.590607) point directly to the primary shoreline collecting area.
road_conditions: Paved roads to lake access area. Final approach to parking may be gravel or unpaved but passable by standard vehicles.
parking_notes: Public parking area available near lake with multiple pullouts and informal parking along access roads. Capacity typically moderate with available space except peak recreation weekends.
collection_rules: Rockhounding for personal use is permitted on publicly accessible shoreline. No commercial collection. No motorized equipment or excavation. Respect posted area boundaries and any seasonal closures related to wildlife management or recreation restrictions. Pack out all trash and minimize environmental impact.
rules:
Personal use only
No commercial collection
No motorized equipment or excavation
Respect posted boundaries and seasonal closures
Pack out all trash
Minimize environmental disturbance
nearest_services: Williamsport, approximately 10 miles south, has full services including gas stations, restaurants, and grocery stores.
accessibility_notes: Relatively flat terrain with gentle slopes accessible to most hikers and families. Shoreline approach is straightforward with minimal elevation change. Sturdy footwear recommended due to variable rocky and sandy footing. No wheelchair accessibility to the shoreline itself, but parking areas are accessible.

faq.question: What gemstones can I find rockhounding at Beaver Lake in Pennsylvania?
faq.answer: Beaver Lake is known for quartz specimens including clear, milky white, and smoky varieties, along with feldspar fragments and mica sheets. These minerals are common in the Appalachian basement rocks that surround the lake and are continuously exposed by water erosion along the shoreline.

faq.question: Is rockhounding at Beaver Lake free and do I need a permit?
faq.answer: Yes, rockhounding at Beaver Lake is free and no permit is required for personal use collection. The shoreline is publicly accessible, making it an affordable option for casual collectors and families new to the hobby.

faq.question: What is the best time of year to go rockhounding at Beaver Lake near Williamsport?
faq.answer: April through October offers the most comfortable conditions for shoreline collecting. Warmer months provide easier access and better visibility, though spring and fall can be excellent after seasonal weather events churn up fresh mineral material.

faq.question: Can I bring my children and dog rockhounding at Beaver Lake Pennsylvania?
faq.answer: Absolutely. Beaver Lake is extremely family and dog-friendly with easy terrain and minimal hazards. The gentle slopes and accessible shoreline make it ideal for children ages 6 and up to learn rockhounding basics without strenuous hiking.

faq.question: What tools and equipment do I need for rockhounding at Beaver Lake?
faq.answer: Bring a small geology pick or flathead screwdriver for working exposed mineral deposits, a mesh collecting bag, sturdy boots for rocky footing, and a field guide to identify feldspar and quartz varieties. A magnifying glass helps examine specimens for clear identification.

faq.question: How far is Beaver Lake rockhounding site from Williamsport and is it beginner-friendly?
faq.answer: Beaver Lake is approximately 10 miles north of Williamsport, making it easily accessible for day trips. It is highly beginner-friendly with short walking distances from parking, flat terrain, and common minerals that are straightforward to identify in the field.

meta_description: Find quartz, feldspar, and mica at Beaver Lake near Williamsport, PA. Free public rockhounding on scenic shoreline. Perfect for families and beginners.
